The knight forks the king and rook, winning the exchange. Then Bg4+ forces the king further, and Qxd1 wins White's queen. An absolute classic of chess tactics.

variations.name: Albin Countergambit: Normal Development (Nf3 System)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 Nf3 Nc6 Nbd2 Bg4 a3 Qd7 b4 Nge7 Bb2 Ng6 g3 Be7 Bg2 O-O O-O f6 exf6 Bxf6 Nb3 Rae8 Re1 Bxf3 Bxf3 Qd5 Bxd5+ Re6 Bxe6+
variations.explanation: The standard line where White plays Nf3 and Nbd2 to handle the d4 pawn. Black develops with Nc6, Bg4, and Nge7. The position is dynamic — White targets the d4 pawn while Black aims for kingside play. The f6 break opens lines for Black's pieces.

variations.name: Albin Countergambit: d3 Push Disruption
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 Nf3 Nc6 a3 Nge7 b4 Ng6 Bb2 a5 b5 Ncxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 e3 Be6 exd4 Nxc4 Bxc4 Bxc4 Qa4 Bd3 Qc4 Bb4+ Qxb4 Qe7+ Qxe7+ Kxe7
variations.explanation: The d3 push is one of Black's most dangerous weapons. After the pawn advances to d3, it disrupts White's entire coordination. In this line, the d2+ check wins back material, and after Qxd4+ and Qxa1, Black has recovered everything with an active position.

variations.name: Albin Countergambit: e3 Line (Non-Lasker)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 e3 Bb4+ Bd2 dxe3 Bxb4 exf2+ Kxf2 Qxd1 Nc3 Qd4+ Ke1 Nc6 Nb5 Bg4 Nf3 Bxf3 gxf3 Qe3+ Be2 O-O-O Rg1 Nge7 Rg3 Qb6 a3 Nd4 a4 Ndf5
variations.explanation: When White avoids the Lasker Trap by playing Kxf2 instead of Ke2, the position is messy but playable. White has the bishop pair but the king is exposed. Black's active pieces and centralized queen provide enough compensation. Both sides have chances in this double-edged middlegame.

variations.name: Albin Countergambit: Fianchetto System
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 Nf3 Nc6 g3 Bg4 Bg2 Qd7 O-O Nge7 Nbd2 Ng6 Nb3 Ngxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Nbxd4 c5 Nc2 Bh3 f3 Bxg2 Kxg2 Nc6 Ne3 Be7 b3 O-O Bb2 Rad8
variations.explanation: White fianchettos to handle the d4 pawn in a more positional way. Black develops naturally with Bg4, Nge7, and Ng6. After recovering the pawn on e5, Black has equal chances. The fianchetto bishop on g2 is strong but Black's centralized pieces provide counterplay.

trapLines.name: Lasker Trap: Full Brilliancy
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 e3 Bb4+ Bd2 dxe3 Bxb4 exf2+ Ke2 fxg1=N+ Ke3 Qxd1 Nc3 Bf5 Nxd1 Nc6 Nc3 O-O-O Bd3 Rxd3+ Kf4 Ne2+ Kxf5 Nh6+ Ke4 Nxc3+ Bxc3 Rd4+
trapLines.explanation: The Lasker Trap at its most spectacular — after the knight promotion, White's king goes on an insane journey. After Ke3, Black plays Qxd1 winning the queen. If White tries Kf4, the sequence g5+ Kxg5 Nf6+ continues the king hunt. The checks never stop and White's position collapses completely.

trapLines.name: d3 Advance Disruption Trap
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 Nf3 Nc6 a3 d3 e3 Bg4 Be2 dxe2 Qxe2 Bxf3 Qxf3 Qd3 Qxc6+ bxc6 e4 Qxe4+ Be3 Qxe3+ fxe3 Bb4+ axb4 O-O-O O-O Nf6 exf6 gxf6
trapLines.explanation: The d3 push disrupts White's piece coordination completely. After dxe2 Qxe2, White has recovered the pawn but the damage is done — the bishop pair is gone and Black has a development lead. After Qd3, Black trades queens and reaches a favorable endgame with active pieces.

trapLines.name: Bishop Pin Trap
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 Nf3 Nc6 Bg5 Be7 Bxe7 Qxe7 Nbd2 Bg4 a3 O-O-O b4 d3 e3 Nxe5 Nxe5 Qxe5 Bxd3 Bxd1 Kxd1 Rxd3 Ke2 Rxd2+ Kxd2 Qd4+ Ke1 Qxa1+
trapLines.explanation: The bishop pin on g4 combined with the d3 push creates tactical havoc. After Bg5 Be7 Bxe7 Qxe7, the d3 advance disrupts everything. The sequence of captures leads to Black winning decisive material — the rook sacrifice on d3 followed by the queen checks wins the game.

trapLines.name: e3 Queen Trap Variation
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 e3 Bb4+ Bd2 dxe3 Bxb4 exf2+ Ke2 fxg1=N+ Rxg1 Bg4+ Ke3 Qd4+ Kxd4 Nc6+ Kd5 O-O-O+ Ke4 Nf6+ exf6 Rd4+ Qxd4 Bf5+ Kxf5 Nxd4+ Ke4 Re8+ Kxd4 Re4+
trapLines.explanation: After the Lasker Trap knight promotion, if White tries to develop with Nf3, the Bg4+ pin keeps the pressure on. After Nc6 and Qd3+, the queen invades to the heart of White's position. The knight jumps to b4 and then d3, creating forks that win material.

trapLines.name: Morozevich's Attacking Idea
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 Nf3 Nc6 a3 Bf5 b4 d3 e3 a5 Bb2 axb4 axb4 Rxa1 Bxa1 Nxb4 Bxd3 Bxd3 Qa4+ Nc6 Qxc6+ bxc6 Bd4 Bb4+ Nc3 Bxc3+ Bxc3 Bxc4 e4 Qd2+
trapLines.explanation: Morozevich's attacking idea features aggressive queenside play. After a5 and axb4, Black opens the a-file for the rook. The Rxa1 Bxa1 exchange gives Black a powerful passed d-pawn. The d3 pawn is a constant thorn that ties White down to defense.

warningLines.name: White's Accurate Nf3-Nbd2 Refutation
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 Nf3 Nc6 Nbd2 Nge7 a3 Ng6 b4 a5 b5 Ncxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Nb3 Be6 Nxd4 Bd7 e3 Qf6 Be2 Bc5 O-O O-O Bb2 Qg6 Bf3 Rad8 Nb3 Bf5 Nxc5 Nd3
warningLines.explanation: White's precise Nf3-Nbd2 system methodically dismantles the Albin. The d4 pawn is blockaded and eventually won. After b4 and a3, White controls the queenside. Without the d4 pawn, Black has sacrificed material for nothing. This is why top players rarely employ the Albin.

warningLines.name: e3 Without Falling for Lasker
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 e3 Bb4+ Bd2 dxe3 Bxb4 exf2+ Kxf2 Qxd1 Nc3 Qd4+ Ke1 Qxe5+ Be2 Nc6 Nf3 Qd4 Nd5 Nge7 Bxe7 Nxe7 Nxc7+ Kf8 Ne6+ Bxe6 Nxd4 Bxc4
warningLines.explanation: When White plays Kxf2 instead of Ke2, the Lasker Trap is avoided completely. The position is messy but manageable for White. The bishop pair and active knights give White chances. Black's queen is active but alone — the position requires very precise play from both sides.

warningLines.name: Alapin Variation: White Plays 4.e4
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e5 dxe5 d4 e4 Nc6 f4 f6 exf6 Nxf6 Nf3 Bb4+ Bd2 Bc5 Bd3 Ng4 O-O Nxh2 Kxh2 Qh4+ Kg1 Bg4 Bc2 Bxf3 Qxf3 Qxf4 Qxf4 O-O-O Qxc7+ Kxc7
warningLines.explanation: White's e4 response creates a powerful center immediately. After f4, the center pawns are imposing. While Black gets some tactical chances with Ng4 and Qh4+, accurate play by White neutralizes the threats. The resulting position favors White's superior pawn structure.

name: Benko Gambit
pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 g6 g3 Bg7 Kg2 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Re1 Qa5 Qc2 Rfb8 Rb1 Nb6 Nd2 Nc4 Nxc4 Qxc3
overview: The Benko Gambit is the most positionally sound gambit in chess — Black sacrifices a pawn not for a quick attack, but for long-lasting queenside pressure that can grind White down over the entire game. After b5 and a6, Black opens the a and b files and gets sustained initiative with rooks, bishops, and queens all participating in the pressure. Unlike most gambits, the Benko works beautifully in the endgame too — those open files and active pieces give Black compensation even without queens on the board.
keyIdeas: Open the a-file and b-file for rooks to create lasting queenside pressure that never goes away
Fianchetto the bishop to g7 — it controls the long diagonal and supports queenside operations with latent power
Black's initiative is structural, not tactical — the pressure lasts into the endgame because open files don't disappear
The a6 and b5 pawn sacrifice opens lines that can never be closed — this is permanent compensation
traps: If White tries to hold both extra pawns greedily, Black's rooks on a8 and b8 combined with Bg7 create irresistible pressure on the a2 and b2 targets.
After Bg7 and O-O, Black's bishop on g7 often has a crushing tactical shot on the long diagonal involving Bxa1 or a discovered attack.
White often loses the b5 pawn back anyway, but in a worse position — the time spent defending it costs development tempi.
warnings: If White declines with 4.Nd2 or 4.a4, the standard gambit lines don't arise — have a backup plan prepared
Don't play the Benko if White avoids d5 — the gambit only works in the Benoni pawn structure
If White gets a strong central pawn majority rolling with e4-e5, the queenside pressure may not provide enough compensation

variations.name: Benko Gambit: Fully Accepted (King Walk)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 g6 g3 Bg7 Kg2 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Re1 Qa5 Qc2 Rfb8 Rb1 Nb6 Nd2 Nc4 Nxc4 Qxc3 Qxc3 Nxe4 Qxg7+ Kxg7 Bh6+ Kxh6
variations.explanation: The main line where White accepts both pawns with the king walk maneuver. After Kxf1, g3, and Kg2, White's king is safe on g2. Black plays the standard Benko plan with Bg7, O-O, Nbd7, and rooks to the a and b files. The resulting middlegame is one of the most strategically rich in chess.

variations.name: Benko Gambit: Fianchetto System
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 g3 d6 Bg2 g6 Nf3 Bg7 O-O O-O Nc3 Nbd7 Re1 Qa5 Qc2 Rfb8 Rb1 Nb6 Nd2 Nc4 Nxc4 Bxc4 a4 Ra6 e4 Rba8 Be3 Nd7
variations.explanation: White fianchettos the kingside bishop, creating a solid but passive setup. Black responds with the standard plan of Bg7, O-O, and rooks to the open files. The Nc4 maneuver targets the b2 and a3 weaknesses. After Ra6 and Rba8, the doubled rooks create irresistible pressure.

variations.name: Benko Gambit: Declined with Nd2
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 Nd2 bxc4 Nxc4 d6 e4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O Ba6 Qc2 Bxc4 Bxc4 Nbd7 a4 Qa5 Bd2 Qb6 Bc3 Rfb8 Bd3 a5 Ra2 Nxe4
variations.explanation: White declines the full gambit with Nd2, recovering the pawn on c4. Black still gets an active position with d6, Bg7, and Ba6. The Benko spirit lives on even in declined lines — Black's piece placement is natural and the open a-file provides lasting counterplay.

variations.name: Benko Gambit: Modern with bxa6 g6
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 g6 Nc3 Bxa6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 d6 g3 Bg7 Kg2 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Re1 Qa5 Qc2 Rfb8 Nd2 Nb6 Rb1 Nc4 Nxc4 Qxc3 Qxc3 Nxe4 Qxg7+ Kxg7 Bh6+ Kxh6
variations.explanation: A modern move order where Black plays g6 before Bxa6. This can transpose to the main lines but gives Black some flexibility. The key ideas remain the same — fianchetto, trade the light-squared bishop, and apply pressure on the a and b files with rooks.

variations.name: Benko Gambit: e6 Break System
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 Nf3 g6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 Bg7 g3 O-O Kg2 Nbd7 Re1 Qa5 Qc2 Rfb8 a4 Ne8 h3 Nc7 Be3 Nb5 Nxb5 Rxb5 Nd2 e6 dxe6 fxe6
variations.explanation: The e6 break is a powerful pawn lever that opens the position when White has blocked the queenside. After dxe6 fxe6, the e-file opens and Black's rooks become extremely active. Combined with the already-open a and b files, Black has pressure on three files simultaneously.

trapLines.name: a-File Pressure Trap
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 g6 Nf3 Bg7 g3 O-O Kg2 Nbd7 Re1 Qa5 Qc2 Rfb8 Rb1 Nb6 b3 Ra7 Bb2 Rba8 Ra1 Nbxd5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxd5 e5 Nf6+ Bxf6
trapLines.explanation: The doubled rooks on the a-file create tremendous pressure. After Ra7 and Rba8, every piece on a2 and a4 is a target. The knight maneuver to b5 attacks the a3 and c3 squares. White is tied down to defense of the queenside while Black can improve at leisure.

trapLines.name: Nc6 Fork Trick
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 Nf3 Nxd5 Nxd5 Qa5+ Bd2 Qxd2+ Nxd2 e5 Nf6+ gxf6 Qa4+ Nc6 Qxc6+ Ke7 Qxd6+ Kxd6 Ne4+ Kd5 O-O-O+ Kxe4 Rd4+ cxd4 f3+ Kd5 e4+ dxe3 Bc4+ Bxc4
trapLines.explanation: After White pushes e5 too aggressively, Black can exploit the weakened center. The exchange sacrifice followed by Nc5 and Na4 creates devastating threats. The knight on c3 forks multiple targets and Black's queenside majority becomes a decisive advantage.

trapLines.name: Fianchetto Battery Trap
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 Nf3 g6 g3 Bg7 Bg2 O-O O-O Nbd7 Qc2 Ra7 Rd1 Qa8 Bf1 Qxd5 Nxd5 Nxd5 Rxd5 e5 Rxe5 dxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Qxc5 Nf3+ exf3 Bxb2 Qxf8+ Kxf8
trapLines.explanation: The fianchetto battery with Bg7, Ra7, and Qa8 targets the a2 pawn. When White's pieces are tied down, the Bxa2 sacrifice opens the floodgates. After Nxa2 Qxa2 Rxa2, Black has a powerful passed a-pawn and active rooks. The queenside pressure becomes unstoppable.

trapLines.name: Queenside Avalanche
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 g6 g3 Bg7 Kg2 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Re1 Qa5 Qc2 Rfb8 Rb1 Nb6 Nd2 Nc4 Nxc4 Qxc3 Qxc3 Nxe4 Qxg7+ Kxg7 Bh6+ Kxh6 Rxe4 e5 Nxe5 dxe5
trapLines.explanation: A typical Benko endgame where Black's queenside pressure becomes overwhelming. Even after queens are traded, the doubled rooks on the a-file combined with the Bg7 on the long diagonal create a vise. White's extra pawn is meaningless when every piece is tied down to defense.

trapLines.name: Rook Lift to a6
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 Nf3 g6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 Bg7 Kg1 O-O Ne2 Nbd7 a4 Ra6 e5 dxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Be3 Nf3+ gxf3 Qxd5 Qxd5 Nxd5 Bxc5 e5 Bxf8 e4 fxe4 Kxf8
trapLines.explanation: The rook lift Ra6 is a classic Benko maneuver. The rook supports the a-file pressure and can swing to b6 or c6 if needed. After Qa8 and Rba8, the doubled rooks are devastating. The knight maneuver Nc7-Nb5 targets the a-pawn and creates additional threats.

warningLines.name: White's Central Breakthrough (e4-e5)
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 g6 Nf3 Bg7 g3 O-O Kg2 Nbd7 Re1 Ng4 h3 Nge5 Nxe5 Nxe5 f4 Nd7 e5 dxe5 fxe5 Nxe5 Bf4 Nd3 Qxd3 Qxd5+ Qf3 Qxf3+ Kxf3
warningLines.explanation: If White manages to push e4-e5 at the right moment, the central majority comes alive. The pawns advance and open lines for White's pieces. This is the main danger in the Benko — if White plays actively in the center instead of passively defending, the gambit pawn deficit becomes real.

warningLines.name: Benko Declined: a4 Line
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 a4 bxc4 Nc3 g6 e4 d6 Bxc4 Bg7 Nf3 O-O O-O Ba6 Bxa6 Nxa6 Re1 Nc7 Bf4 Rb8 Qc2 Nfe8 a5 Rb5 Nd2 f5 exf5 Rxf5 Nc4 Nxd5 Nxd5 Rxd5
warningLines.explanation: White declines the full gambit with a4, recovering the pawn while maintaining the center. After Nc3, e4, and Bxc4, White has a solid position. Black still has some activity but the characteristic Benko pressure is greatly reduced because the a-file isn't fully open.

warningLines.name: King's Indian Attack Setup
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 f3 g6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 Bg7 g3 O-O Kg2 Nbd7 Nge2 Ne5 Be3 Qa5 Qd2 Rfb8 a4 Nfd7 Nc1 Nb6 Nb3 Qa6 f4 Nbd7 Nd4 cxd4 Bxd4 Nc5
warningLines.explanation: White's King's Indian Attack setup with f3, e4, and Nge2 creates a fortress. The center is solid and the king is safe on g2. Black's queenside pressure is contained and the extra pawn gives White a long-term advantage. This patient approach is one of the best ways to handle the Benko.
